one_line_takeaway: Contrary to the folk assumption that model compression protects privacy, mixed-supervision knowledge distillation can preserve or amplify membership leakage in student LLMs; capacity-limiting tweaks (Bottleneck Projection, NoNorm) reduce member-specific MIA success while NoNorm even preserves utility.
key_finding: Distillation does not consistently improve privacy. On aggregate MIA accuracy across 36 pair/attack cells the teacher is lower in 15, the student in 17, tie in 4 (sign test p=0.43, fail to reject the null that teachers are not consistently safer); on member-specific accuracy the teacher is lower in 18, student in 16, tie in 2 (p=0.432). Member-specific MIA accuracy rises for the student by 9.81% (Pythia family) and 2.29% (Llama family) versus the teacher. A striking case: under the Reference-model attack on Pythia, the student has LOWER aggregate accuracy (0.579 vs teacher 0.648) yet HIGHER member-specific accuracy (0.636 vs 0.620), showing aggregate metrics can hide member risk. Mitigations work: non-vulnerable-only distillation cuts MIA accuracy by 75.02% on the vulnerable subset Dv and 35.20% on members D (comparable on non-members) but badly hurts utility (member perplexity 436.01 vs 118.21 for full-data distillation). The architectural fixes reduce aggregate member-side attack accuracy by 9.45% (Bottleneck Projection), 4.71% (NoNorm), and 4.49% (All), with small changes on non-members; crucially NoNorm improves utility (member perplexity 52.99 vs 68.96 for unprotected None), and the combined 'All' variant achieves the best utility (51.68) while still suppressing member leakage, recovering the utility Bottleneck Projection alone sacrifices (138.44).
core_contribution: The paper makes two contributions of its own. (1) A systematic empirical study of how knowledge distillation affects membership inference attack (MIA) vulnerability in LLMs, evaluated across six teacher-student model pairs and six MIA methods. Its central and counter-intuitive finding is that distilled student models do NOT reliably exhibit lower MIA success than their teachers, and can show substantially higher member-specific attack success, contradicting the common assumption that compression improves privacy. The authors attribute this to the mixed-supervision distillation objective (ground-truth cross-entropy + KL to the teacher's soft predictions): on already-vulnerable training points, the teacher's predictions align with ground truth, reinforcing overconfident student behavior that widens the member/non-member separability; on non-vulnerable points the two signals diverge, providing no clear privacy benefit. (2) Three mitigation methods, of which the last two are the paper's own novel proposals for this setting: (a) restricting distillation to non-vulnerable data points, (b) a low-dimensional Bottleneck Projection in the feed-forward network that caps representational capacity, and (c) NoNorm, replacing layer normalization with an element-wise affine transform (gamma*h + beta) to avoid encoding training-data statistics. NOTE: the six MIA methods used (ReCaLL, Loss/Yeom, Zlib/Carlini, Reference-model, Min-K%, Min-K%++) are all prior work, not contributions of this paper; NoNorm as a latency technique originates from Sun et al. (MobileBERT) and is repurposed here as a privacy defense.
threat_model: A membership inference adversary who, given a target data point d and query access to a trained LLM, computes a per-point confidence score (e.g. loss, zlib-normalized loss, Min-K% average log-likelihood, or a likelihood-ratio against a reference/shadow model) and thresholds it (threshold chosen via ROC analysis to maximize balanced accuracy) to decide whether d was in the training set. The paper's specific concern is whether distilled student LLMs leak training membership as much as or more than their teacher models, with member-specific true-positive rate treated as the practically dominant risk (following Carlini et al. 2022). Defender goal: reduce MIA success (especially on members) while preserving language-model utility (perplexity).
limitations: Restricted to three model families (Pythia, Gemma 2, Llama) and a narrow set of datasets (Pile ArXiv, WikiMIA), so generalization to other architectures, distillation protocols, domains, languages, or multimodal data is untested. The two architectural defenses and all mitigation experiments are evaluated only on the Pythia/DistilPythia family (30-epoch training) due to compute constraints, and on small member/non-member subsets (Tables 3/5 use dozens of points, e.g. accuracies at 1/81 granularity), limiting statistical power. Mitigations are purely empirical with no formal guarantee; the authors explicitly do not combine them with DP-SGD or other mechanisms that provide provable privacy. Bottleneck Projection alone degrades utility, and the reported member-specific improvements (2-10%) and sign-test p-values (~0.43) are modest. NoNorm's privacy benefit is argued mechanistically (avoiding normalization statistics) rather than proven.

datasets_used: The Pile - ArXiv subset (training data for Pythia and Llama 3.1 8B)
WikiMIA (training/eval for Gemma 2 family; provides built-in non-member split)
WebInstructSub-prometheus (non-member set for Pythia and Llama families; released May 2024, after model cutoffs)
quantitative_results: Aggregate MIA (Table 1, 36 cells): teacher lower in 15, student lower in 17, tie in 4; sign test p=0.43 (fail to reject H0)
Member-specific MIA (Table 2): teacher lower in 18, student lower in 16, tie in 2; sign test p=0.432
Member-specific MIA accuracy increase for student vs teacher: +9.81% (Pythia family), +2.29% (Llama family)
Reference-model attack on Pythia: student aggregate 0.579 vs teacher 0.648 (lower) but student member-specific 0.636 vs teacher 0.620 (higher)
Non-vulnerable-only distillation (DistilPythia): -75.02% MIA accuracy on vulnerable subset Dv, -35.20% on member set D (Table 3), averaged over six attacks
Non-vulnerable-only distillation utility cost (Table 4): member perplexity 436.01 vs 118.21 for full-data distillation; vulnerable-subset perplexity 972.96 vs 94.45
Architectural defenses cut aggregate member-side attack accuracy by 9.45% (Bottleneck Projection), 4.71% (NoNorm), 4.49% (All) (Table 5)
Member perplexity (Table 6): None 68.96, Bottleneck Projection 138.44, NoNorm 52.99, All 51.68
Bottleneck dimension ablation (Table 9): member perplexity best at B=768 (134.99), worst at B=96 (218.67); B=384 gives 138.44; smaller B generally lowers MIA accuracy (Table 8)
baselines_compared: Teacher model vs distilled student model (six pairs: Pythia/DistilPythia; Gemma 2 27B vs 2B, 2B-distilled, 9B; Llama 3.1 8B vs 3.2 1B, 3.2 3B)
Six MIA methods (all prior work): ReCaLL, Loss (Yeom et al.), Zlib (Carlini et al.), Reference-model likelihood-ratio, Min-K%, Min-K%++
Distillation on non-vulnerable data only vs distillation on full dataset
Student variants: None (standard distillation) vs Bottleneck Projection (B=384) vs NoNorm vs All (combined)
